data.name: Angus Gunn
data.description: Gunn took the unusual step of publicly admitting he may have made a transfer mistake, having joined Forest on a one-year deal from Norwich City last summer. By the time Gunn reached the October international camp, he was yet to play any club football. “I went into a situation at the club where I thought I'd be getting, obviously not regular game time, but some game time in terms of cups and things like that," he said. “It's not really materialised, which I'm disappointed about.” Gunn’s first domestic action of the season came from the bench on 1 February. Gunn has, though, routinely performed well for Scotland despite his club inaction. Gunn’s father is the Norwich City legend and former Scotland goalkeeper Bryan Gunn. Earlier in his career, there was a theory Angus had eyes on the England squad. “His name is Angus Fraser James Gunn – he was always destined to play for Scotland,” insisted Bryan.

data.name: Aaron Hickey
data.description: A player pursued by Bayern Munich after bursting on to the scene as a 16-year-old at Hearts. Hickey has a tendency to do things his own way. Having moved from the Hearts academy to that of Celtic, he returned to Tynecastle through belief he would have more opportunity of first-team football in Edinburgh. Hickey was proved right then and proved right once more; he snubbed the Bavarian giants to sign for Bologna, where his rise continued. But for injuries, it is hardly a sign of disrespect to Brentford to suggest he would be at one of the Premier League’s bigger clubs. Equally comfortable on left or right foot. Has emerged as the best option for Scotland at right-back. A tenacious, now powerful defender with technique which almost feels wasted in defence.

data.name: Andy Robertson
data.description: Robertson remains in close pursuit of Kenny Dalglish’s record of 102 caps for Scotland. The left-back’s story is widely known; having been released by Celtic, he stacked shelves in Asda and played as an amateur for Queen’s Park. “Life is rubbish at this age with no money,” posted Robertson on social media in 2012. Seven years later, he was a Champions League winner. Robertson’s career took off at Dundee United and Hull City but was placed on another level by Jürgen Klopp. Captaincy of Scotland initially looked a heavy burden for Robertson but he has looked comfortable in the role for a long time now. One of a number of Scotland players who may privately sense this marks their final World Cup opportunity.

data.name: Scott McTominay
data.description: Did he launch himself 8ft, 18ft or 88ft into the air before planting an outrageous overhead kick beyond the reach of Kasper Schmeichel? Who cares? McTominay’s opening goal against Denmark set the tone for a remarkable night as Scotland ended almost three decades of World Cup hurt. Alex McLeish’s greatest gift to his nation during a second spell as the Scotland manager was convincing McTominay to don navy blue. Without McLeish’s refusal to take no for an answer, the Lancaster-born McTominay could be an England player by now. Instead, he is idolised by the Tartan Army. There is lauding, too, in Naples, after he was part of the 2024-25 scudetto-winning squad. “If Diego was God, then Scott is Jesus,” insisted Diego Maradona Jr, in reference to the status his father and McTominay have secured in the eyes of Napoli fans. McTominay will arrive at the World Cup with a point to prove, having disappointed in Euro 2024.

data.name: Grant Hanley
data.description: Doubts were raised about Hanley’s World Cup involvement after he missed March’s friendlies when recovering from injury. Yet the centre-half has been a regular under Steve Clarke and has rarely let his country down: he started two of Scotland’s three games in Euro 2024. Hanley was central to a rumpus early in the last campaign. Motherwell’s manager, Jens Berthel Askou, was accused of disrespect towards the veteran after Hibs were defeated 2-0 in Lanarkshire. Askou said Motherwell prevailed after “cutting off their supply from their midfielders leaving Hanley to bring the ball forward”. Cue suggestions Askou had needlessly highlighted Hanley’s shortcomings.

data.name: Kieran Tierney
data.description: The stunning left-footed strike that nudged Scotland ahead of Denmark on the night of World Cup qualification felt hugely appropriate for Tierney. He remains a factor on the big stage. There should be no shedding of tears for a player who has served Celtic – twice – and Arsenal but it is undeniable that physical problems have prevented Tierney from sitting among Scotland’s all-time greats. How he will relish a World Cup. Tierney once ran through crowds outside Hampden to join Scottish Cup final celebrations with his Celtic teammates. He had suffered a broken jaw and teeth early in the game but raced back from hospital to play a part in the party.

data.name: John McGinn
data.description: It remains extraordinary in a way that McGinn has forged a top-level career at all. In 2015, a spot of high jinks at St Mirren’s training ground led to his teammate Steven Thompson thring a spiked pole at McGinn. The pole cut McGinn deeply and tore a muscle. “There was a lot of blood,” McGinn said. “I just remember looking down and being glad the pole came back out.” Whether through fear over the impact of that incident or otherwise, there were a shortage of takers when McGinn decided to leave Paisley. Hibernian took a chance and the rest is history. McGinn excelled in Edinburgh and has become a fan favourite at Aston Villa. The Tartan Army hold the midfielder nicknamed Meatball in similarly lofty esteem.

data.name: Tyler Fletcher
data.description: A horror moment for Billy Gilmour triggered the stuff of dreams for Fletcher. The midfielder had been brought in among five youngsters to boost training numbers as Scotland prepared for their friendly against Curaçao. Gilmour sustained a knee injury in the first half of that game, ruling the Napoli player out of the World Cup. Fletcher had impressed in training and during the second half of the Curaçao game to usurp Lennon Miller, Andy Irving and Connor Barron when named as Gilmour’s replacement. The son of the former Scotland captain Darren Fletcher, Tyler’s brother Jack also plays for Manchester United but represents England at youth level. Could Tyler’s elevation trigger an international rethink from Jack?

data.name: Lyndon Dykes
data.description: Scotland have lost players born in the country to other nations – James McCarthy, Aiden McGeady and Harry Souttar are examples – but Dykes took the opposite approach. He lived in Australia until visiting Dumfries, from where his parents hail, with Queen of the South sufficiently impressed during a prolific spell in their age-group teams to bring Dykes back to Scotland permanently. He later had a straight choice between representing Australia and Scotland. Dykes “went with my heart and my gut”. A horrible striker to play against and one who has delivered big moments for Scotland. Will now seek to make up for lost time after being devastated to miss Euro 2024 through injury.

data.name: Ché Adams
data.description: Emulated the Scottish icon Denis Law by scoring his 10th goal for Torino last October. Steve Clarke believes Adams is underappreciated by observers of the Scotland team, given the forward’s level of selfless running. The movement of Adams is key to the Scots having a strong scoring threat from midfield. Adams played for England at under-20 level and was also eligible to represent Antigua. He committed to Scotland shortly before Euro 2020 after long-term speculation about his international future and is likely to be Clarke’s first-choice centre-forward in the World Cup. Adams backed Scotland’s fans after full-time booing when Belarus were narrowly seen off during qualifying. “I think they were right,” he said. “It was some poor decision-making, not concentrating in key moments of the game. We need to do better.”

data.name: Ryan Christie
data.description: Christie’s father, Charlie, was a hugely talented midfielder who never played on the stage his ability deserved despite a brief stint at Celtic. Charlie and Ryan stand out in the domain in hailing from Inverness, a Scottish city that has curiously offered little by way of blue-chip footballers over several decades. A mainstay in Steve Clarke’s squads and a useful goal threat, Christie’s own career at Celtic looked over before circumstances afforded him an opportunity he gleefully seized in late 2018. Three years later, the Premier League beckoned. Was memorably reduced to tears during a live television interview as Scotland reached Euro 2020. Charlie, so taken by the depiction of that moment in a Guardian cartoon strip, ordered a framed copy for his son’s birthday.

data.name: Liam Kelly
data.description: Took a risk when swapping Motherwell for Rangers in the summer of 2024, given he was trading guaranteed first-team football for a likely understudy position at Ibrox. Kelly has indeed played second fiddle to Jack Butland but has remained involved in the Scotland scene. He is a hugely popular character within the Scotland dressing room. He was never likely to play in Euro 2024 but earned a spot in the finals squad, as again now, partly through the role he plays in squad camaraderie. Kelly’s two brothers, Sean and Shay, also play league football in Scotland. Liam’s Scotland debut, against France in a friendly at the age of 27, was an emotional moment for all concerned.

data.name: Jack Hendry
data.description: Hendry was tipped for great things by Brendan Rodgers back in 2018. “He has the quality to play for Celtic and the international team for the next 10 years,” said Rodgers. “His profile suits the highest level. He's quick and can defend and it's important you can deal with the ball.” Hendry’s path has not been exactly as Rodgers predicted but a move to Saudi Arabia has not diminished Steve Clarke’s faith in the centre-back. Rodgers’ words seemed to resonate with Steven Gerrard, who took Hendry to the club. Hendry had earlier excelled in the Belgian league. When Scotland opened the last Euros, Hendry was in the heart of the defence. While international opportunity has been limited since, it would be a surprise if Hendry does not feature at all in the World Cup.

data.name: Ross Stewart
data.description: His nickname among fans is undeniably the finest in the Scotland squad: the Loch Ness Drogba. We will never know for sure what part fate played in Stewart’s World Cup call-up. His end of season form with Southampton means he merited his place on the flight to North America but the injury sustained by Tommy Conway, who had returned to the Scotland squad in March, seemed to accelerate Stewart’s chances. The Southampton man had not featured for the national team since 2022. A decade ago, he was a non-league player in Ayrshire. His ascent since leaving St Mirren in 2018 has been sharp despite serious injury woes of his own. Via Ross County and Sunderland, Stewart became a £10m player. At one point, Albion Rovers bought him for just £1,500.

data.name: John Souttar
data.description: Souttar shared a flat with Andy Robertson and Ryan Gauld as the trio were youth players at Dundee United. All three have gone on to impressive things thereafter. Souttar does not remember the World Cup of 1998 but knows his father and other family members travelled to France to follow Scotland. Souttar’s brother, Harry, has already featured in a World Cup for Australia, having switched international allegiance. John’s off-field interests include a coffee shop business. He has recovered from two significant achilles injuries to prolong his football career. Souttar was at the centre of a bizarre row in 2018 after his manager at Hearts, Craig Levein, claimed Dundee United “nearly ruined” the player by deploying him in different positions. United hit back with a fierce statement saying they were “very disappointed” by Levein’s words.

data.name: Dominic Hyam
data.description: Born in Leuchars, Fife, where his father, Steve, was stationed with the Royal Air Force, Hyam was in England before the age of two. “I feel Scottish,” he says. “I've got more Scottish than English in me because my dad's mum is from Dundee originally and mum's dad is from Glasgow. So I'm definitely more Scottish than English.” Waited until 27 to make his Scotland debut but did so in hugely dramatic circumstances. Having trailed Norway in a crucial Euros qualifier in Oslo, Scotland scored twice in the closing four minutes to claim a vital three points. Hyam was thrown on in stoppage time to repel the desperate Norwegians. A consistent, reliable defender who has been trusted by umpteen managers; hence spells spanning more than 100 games at each of Coventry and Blackburn.

data.name: Ben Gannon-Doak
data.description: Gannon-Doak recently revealed how he reconnected with religion after leaving home as a 16-year-old to join Liverpool. “I think God just keeps you grounded and humble because he always tells us that we wouldn't have any of this without him,” he said. “I heavily rely on God to keep me strong.” Gannon-Doak excelled in Celtic’s youth system before being coaxed to Anfield. Jürgen Klopp was a fan. However, a combination of injuries and Klopp’s exit stalled the winger’s progress. He then opted to kickstart his career at Bournemouth. Steve Clarke has sought to temper excitement around Gannon-Doak but the noise is understandable. This player can be a genuine difference-maker for the Scots. He has directness and electric pace, which on one Nations League evening against Croatia visibly terrified the £77m full-back Josko Gvardiol.

data.name: George Hirst
data.description: Hirst’s father, David, made just three appearances for England despite his excellent scoring prowess at Sheffield Wednesday. Alex Ferguson tried and failed to sign Hirst Sr for Manchester United on several occasions. George’s hopes of emulating David at Hillsborough failed to materialise but he has found a home at Portman Road and was involved in back-to-back promotion campaigns as Ipswich were catapulted from League One to the Premier League. “He's a tough character and he's done well for us every season in every division,” said the Ipswich manager, Kieran McKenna. “George has has been a massive part of the success here, an incredible servant for the football club and is still a really important person in the leadership group.”

data.name: Lewis Ferguson
data.description: Ferguson would likely be plying his trade for an Italian giant by now – Juventus and the Milan clubs had credible interest – but for the serious knee injury sustained in April of 2024, which kept him out of the Euros of that summer. “The first thing I asked was: ‘Does this mean I’m missing the Euros?’ and they said yes,” Ferguson said. “That was a difficult moment. I’m not an emotional guy but I just sat there thinking that was my chance to play in a major tournament and it was gone. It had been something I’d always dreamed about so it was difficult. But here we are now.” The following year, he emulated Graeme Souness as Scotsmen to win the Coppa Italia. The Bologna captain hails from a family of classy midfielders; namely his father, Derek, and uncle, Barry. A combative midfielder who provides a goal threat Scotland are yet to properly tap in to.

data.name: Lawrence Shankland
data.description: Activated a break clause in his contract at Hearts a week after their season ended, allowing him to join Rangers on a free transfer. As Hearts brushed shoulders with the title, Shankland as the captain was in superb form. Twelve months earlier, there was debate about whether or not the Edinburgh club were doing the right thing in retaining the striker. Derek McInnes, who had released a young Shankland at Aberdeen, subsequently rejuvenated the same player’s career. Scotland’s finest penalty-area threat, whose all-round game has improved with age. What Shankland lacks in pace he atones for with game intelligence. He was the scorer of the “other” goal on that famous night against Denmark. Shankland’s two-yard tap-in was, however, every bit as vital as its more blistering contemporaries as Scotland qualified for the World Cup.

data.name: Craig Gordon
data.description: The World Cup feels an appropriate end to an extraordinary career that in Scotland terms began in May 2004. The intervening years saw Gordon move for a British transfer record for a goalkeeper (£9m in 2007) and encounter serious injury which looked like curtailing his career. Gordon bounced back to prolong his career; winning a bounty of honours at Celtic, returning to his boyhood club, Hearts, and prolonging his international involvement for much longer than anybody reasonably anticipated. His insane save for Hearts at Dundee in January will go down not only as his finest but one of the best Scottish domestic football has witnessed in living memory. “That is a moment I will treasure,” Gordon said. So, too, one of the few boxes he had left to tick; a World Cup place.

data.name: Nathan Patterson
data.description: The World Cup marks the start of a crucial summer for Patterson. Huge things were expected of the full-back when he swapped Rangers for Everton in 2022 but his career has stalled on Merseyside. Patterson needs a transfer and regular football to belatedly build on earlier promise. Changes of Everton manager and a series of injuries have hampered Patterson since his £10m switch. “I’ve experienced a lot of ups and downs,” he says. “Quite a lot of downs, to be honest, in terms of injuries.” When at Rangers, Patterson was one of a group of players fined by police for attending a flat party amid Covid restrictions. The Scottish FA issued a sanction of their own for the incident.

data.name: Kenny McLean
data.description: It is difficult to imagine McLean doing anything at this World Cup, in fact during the remainder of his career, that matches the events of 18 November at Hampden Park. The Scots were bound for the tournament proper but a nation bit fingernails as McLean strode forward during a counterattack, spotted Kasper Schmeichel off his line and unleashed a shot from halfway that brought the roof off the famous old venue. McLean had topped off a night of epic drama with the goal of his life. This was just reward for one of Steve Clarke’s unsung heroes. McLean does, however, have previous for stepping up on the big occasion; he scored the winning penalties in separate matches as Scotland reached Euro 2020. An unfussy, low-maintenance midfielder held in high regard by Clarke and teammates alike.

data.name: Anthony Ralston
data.description: A slightly controversial pick for Scotland in the sense that he has recently and frequently been nudged into the position of third-choice right-back for Celtic. The player who has edged in front of him, Colby Donovan, is also Scottish. Steve Clarke’s persistence with Ralston is a nod to the continuity the manager insists upon within Scotland squads. A boyhood Celtic fan, Ralston’s career at the club was revived by Ange Postecoglou. He had been nicknamed the Bricklayer, the Brickie or the Holy Brickie by Celtic’s followers; a scenario Ralston took in good spirits. "The fans seem to love it, I find it funny and am happy to go along with it,” he said. “Some of the memes are a good laugh.”

data.name: Findlay Curtis
data.description: Steve Clarke’s appearance at the March meeting of Kilmarnock and Hearts proved vital for Curtis. The Rangers loanee produced a fine performance on the left wing for Kilmarnock, with Clarke calling up Curtis just days later for friendly matches. “I was bursting with pride and told my mum and dad,” Curtis recalled. “My mum started crying.” Scotland are short of wide attacking options, which undoubtedly helped Curtis’s case, with Clarke also keen to demonstrate he has eyes on the next generation after Harvey Barnes rejected Scotland’s overtures. Curtis’s international involvement came late; he only appeared for Scotland Under-21s for the first time last year. Clarke has not unreasonably compared Curtis to Ben Gannon-Doak. The likelihood is Curtis will be given a serious shot at first-team football with Rangers next season. He certainly should be.

data.name: Scott McKenna
data.description: One of a number of Scotland players of this era who have opted to take roads less travelled. With his time at Nottingham Forest coming to an end, McKenna took a loan move to Copenhagen. Stints at Las Palmas and Dinamo Zagreb have followed. “He’s like a grumpy old man. I call him mi abuelo, my grandad,” said McKenna’s former Las Palmas teammate Oli McBurnie. An imposing, left-sided centre-half with more pace than he is often given credit for, McKenna has arguably been underused in the Scottish setup. A Zagreb official insisted Dinamo had rejected bids in excess of £10m for McKenna in January. A decade ago, McKenna was trading a loan at Alloa Athletic for the same at Ayr United.
